one.en: COSTS – Where the wife sought a costs order on an indemnity basis following an Application – Enforcement in which the husband was unsuccessful – Where…

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – COSTS – Where the wife sought a costs order on an indemnity basis following an Application – Enforcement in which the husband was unsuccessful – Where the husband conceded that a costs order should be made but contended that it should be on a party/party basis as agreed or assessed – Where the circumstances are not exceptional such as to warrant an indemnity costs order – Costs ordered in a fixed sum. FAMILY LAW – COSTS – Where the wife seeks the second respondent pay her costs on an indemnity basis of an Application in a Proceeding that resulted in consent orders – Where the second respondent opposed the making of a costs order and contended that each party should bear their own costs – Where the Court is not satisfied there are circumstances justifying departure from the usual order that each party pay their own costs – No order for costs – Application dismissed.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

SCHONELL J:

The wife, husband and others are involved in highly contested and complex financial proceedings pursuant to Pt VIII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”). Joined to the proceedings are 10 respondents. There have been a number of interlocutory applications brought by each of the parties and some context to the proceedings is set out in my judgments identified as:
(a) Reynders & Travere [2025] FedCFamC1F 525 delivered on 7 August 2025;

(b) Reynders & Travere (No 2) [2025] FedCFamC1F 797 delivered on 7 November 2025; and

(c) Reynders & Travere (No 3) [2026] FedCFamC1F 85 delivered on 16 February 2026.

COSTS IN RELATION TO THE APPLICATION – ENFORCEMENT

The current application relates to the wife’s costs consequent upon an Application – Enforcement filed by her on 22 December 2025, which was heard on 9 February 2026. Directions were made for the issue of costs to be determined on the papers, with each party to file written submissions limited to three pages by a certain date.
The ambit of the argument between the parties is summarised as follows:
(a) The wife seeks an order that the husband pay her costs on an indemnity basis. However, the quantum of indemnity costs sought is unclear as the wife’s written submissions filed 18 February 2026 (“wife’s written submissions”) appear to have omitted to insert the amount, recording as follows:
An order for the First Respondent to pay the Applicant’s costs on an indemnity basis, in all of the circumstances, is just. The Court should order that the First Respondent pay the Applicant’s costs of an incidental to the Application – Enforcement of the wife filed on 22 December 2025 in the sum of $[INSERT] within 28 days.

(b) The wife’s written submissions contend that her costs notice filed 8 February 2026 “records the value of the unnecessary costs she has incurred” in bringing about the Application – Enforcement. However, upon review, the costs notice records the total costs incurred in the proceedings to date and the total further work undertaken but not yet billed but does not isolate the costs said to relate solely to the Application – Enforcement.

(c) The husband concedes that an order for costs should be made but submits that the appropriate order is that he pay the wife’s costs of and incidental to the Application – Enforcement, as agreed or assessed.

(d) The husband further submits that, having regard to his financial circumstances and the obligations imposed by the orders made 16 February 2026, any costs order should not be enforceable until the final determination of the proceedings.

Distilled further, the issue is whether the appropriate order is for costs on an indemnity basis or on a party/party basis and whether those costs should be fixed in a lump sum or left to agreement or assessment.
The authorities make it plain that an order for indemnity costs is not one that is made lightly and there should be circumstances of an exceptional kind that warrant costs on an indemnity basis. In Kohan and Kohan [1992] FamCA 116; (1993) FLC 92-340, the Full Court observed at 79,614:
The intent of s117(1) and 117(2) is that in this jurisdiction costs should not follow the event as a matter of course. However, where the justice of the matter so requires, the Court may make such order as the Court considers just. As we have pointed out, the Court may depart from the scale of costs prescribed under the rules. However, the purpose of fixing a scale of costs must be understood to signify that they contain the normal rates of charges. By O. 38 r. 2, the provisions of O. 38 apply to costs ordered to be paid or taxed, and costs payable or to be taxed between solicitor and client. O. 38 r. 7 makes provision for the allowance of additional amounts for complexity, difficulty or novelty and special skill, knowledge or responsibility. Consequently, the Court should not depart lightly from the ordinary rules relating to costs between party and party and the circumstances justifying the departure should be of an exceptional kind. See Degmam v. Wright (No.2) “(supra); Wentworth v. Rogers (No. 5) (1986) 6 NSWLR 534; Hobartville Stud v. Union Insurance Co. (1991) 25 NSWLR at 368 to 370.

Indemnity costs orders are still an exception in this and other jurisdictions. ...
Justice Sheppard in Colgate-Palmolive Company v Cussons Pty Ltd [1993] FCA 536; (1993) 46 FCR 225 observed as follows at 232–234:
It seems to me that the following principles or guidelines can be distilled out of the authorities to which I have referred:
The problem arises in adversary litigation, ie litigation as between parties at arm’s length. Different considerations apply where parties may be found to be entitled to the payment of their costs out of a fund or assets being administered by or under the control of a trustee, liquidator, receiver or person in a like position, eg a government agency or statutory authority.
The ordinary rule is that, where the court orders the costs of one party to litigation to be paid by another party, the order is for payment of those costs on the party and party basis. In this court the provisions of O 62, rr 12 and 19, and the Second Schedule to the rules will apply to the taxation. In many cases the result will be that the amount recovered by the successful party under the Order will fall short of (in many cases well short of) a complete indemnity.
This has been the settled practice for centuries in England. It is a practice which is entrenched in Australia. Either legislation (perhaps in the form of an amendment to rules of Court) or a decision of an intermediate court of appeal or of the High Court would be required to alter it. No doubt any consideration of whether there should be any change in the practice would require the resolution of the competing considerations mentioned by Devlin LJ in Berry v British Transport Commission (supra) and Handley JA in Cachia v Hanes (supra) on the one hand and by Rogers J in Qantas on the other. The relevant passages from the respective judgments have been earlier referred to.
In consequence of the settled practice which exists, the Court ought not usually make an order for the payment of costs on some basis other than the party and party basis. The circumstances of the case must be such as to warrant the Court in departing from the usual course. That has been the view of all judges dealing with applications for payment of costs on the indemnity or some other basis whether here or in England. The tests have been variously put. The Court of Appeal in Andrews v Barnes (supra) at 141 said the Court had a general and discretionary power to award costs as between solicitor and client "as and when the justice of the case might so require". Woodward J in Fountain Selected Meats appears to have adopted what was said by Brandon LJ (as he was) in Preston v Preston (supra) at 637; namely, there should be some special or unusual feature in the case to justify the Court in departing from the ordinary practice. Most judges dealing with the problem have resolved the particular case before them by dealing with the circumstances of that case and rmding in it the presence or absence of factors which would be capable, if they existed, of warranting a departure from the usual rule. But as French J said (at p 8) in Tetijo,"The categories in which the discretion may be exercised are not closed". Davies J expressed (at p 6) similar views in Ragata (supra).
Notwithstanding the fact that that is so, it is useful to note some of the circumstances which have been thought to warrant the exercise of the discretion. I instance the making of allegations of fraud knowing them to be false and the making of irrelevant allegations of fraud (both referred to by Woodward J in Fountain and also by Gummow J in Thors v Weekes [1989] FCA 540; (1989) 92 ALR 131 at 152; evidence of particular misconduct that causes loss of time to the Court and to other parties (French J in Tetijo); the fact that the proceedings were commenced or continued for some ulterior motive (Davies J in Ragata) or in wilful disregard of known facts or clearly established law (Woodward J in Fountain and French J in J-Corp (supra)); the making of allegations which ought never to have been made or the undue prolongation of a case by groundless contentions (Davies J in Ragata); an imprudent refusal of an offer to compromise (eg Messiter v Hutchinson (1987) 10 NSWLR 525; Maitland Hospital v Fisher (No 2) (1992) 27 NSWLR 721 at 724 (Court of Appeal); Crisp v Keng (unreported, Court of Appeal, NSW, Kirby P, Priestley JA, Cripps JA, No 40744/1992, 27 September 1993) and an award of costs on an indemnity basis against a contemnor (eg Megarry V-C in EMI Records (supra)). Other categories of cases are to be found in the reports. Yet others to arise in the future will have different features about them which may justify an order for costs on the indemnity basis. The question must always be whether the particular facts and circumstances of the case in question warrant the making of an order for payment of costs other than on a party and party basis.
It remains to say that the existence of particular facts and circumstances capable of warranting the making of an order for payment of costs, for instance, on the indemnity basis, does not mean that judges are necessarily obliged to exercise their discretion to make such an order. The costs are always in the discretion of the trial judge. Provided that discretion is exercised having regard to the applicable principles and the particular circumstances of the instant case its exercise will not be found to have miscarried unless it appears that the order which has been made involves a manifest error or injustice.
The category of cases in which a court may make an indemnity costs order are not closed and are not limited to those identified by Shepard J. In Yunghanns v Yunghanns [2000] FamCA 681; (2000) FLC 93-029 at 87,471, the Full Court said:
... It will suffice to say that the categories of circumstances which enliven the discretion to award indemnity costs are not closed, and that it is not a condition precedent to the exercise of the discretion that some collateral purpose or species of fraud be established against the party against whom such an order is sought. All that is required is that the Court asked to exercise the discretion be satisfied that some “particular facts and circumstances of the case in question warrant the making of an order for the payment of costs other than on a party and party basis”: per Shepherd J in Colgate-Palmolive Company v Cussons Pty Ltd [1993] FCA 536; (1993) 46 FCR 225 at 233.
Whilst the categories are not closed, the defining characteristic that distinguishes cases in which indemnity costs are ordered is the presences of circumstances of an exceptional kind.
The wife submits that the circumstances of the present matter fall within that category. In her written submissions, the wife contends that the Application – Enforcement was necessitated by the husband’s failure to comply with earlier orders of the court and that the application ought not to have been required. The wife further submits that the husband’s failure to properly engage with the enforcement proceedings created additional difficulty and expense for her, and that in those circumstances the costs incurred by her were “unnecessary costs” brought about by the husband’s conduct.
The husband accepts that he did not comply with Order 8 of the orders made on 7 November 2025 and that the wife ought not to have been required to bring the Application – Enforcement. However, he submits that those circumstances do not warrant an indemnity costs order. He points to the fact that he appeared through senior counsel at the hearing on 9 February 2026, conceded the breach and did not oppose the sale of the Suburb G property beyond the indulgence period sought by him, submitting that this demonstrates an attempt to engage with the court’s processes.
In relation to quantum, the husband submits that the amount claimed by the wife has not been properly established and in any event is excessive and disproportionate to the issues raised in the Application – Enforcement. The husband does not, however, as a measure of proportionality inform the Court as to the quantum of costs incurred by him on the application. The husband notes that the wife’s written submissions do not identify the amount sought.
The husband also submits that the Court should have regard to his financial circumstances, noting that he is already subject to orders requiring payment of $156,000 plus interest and the sale of the Suburb G property. In those circumstances, he submits that any costs order should not be enforceable until the final determination of the proceedings.
It is not in issue that an order for costs should be made. In those circumstances, many of the provisions of s 114UB of the Act are of limited application. The first issue is whether there are exceptional circumstances to warrant an order for indemnity costs. I am not satisfied that there is anything exceptional about the circumstances in this case that would warrant an order on an indemnity basis. The mere fact that the husband was unsuccessful, or that the wife was required to bring the Application – Enforcement due to the husband’s non-compliance, is not of itself sufficient to justify an order for indemnity costs.
Nor am I satisfied that the husband’s conduct in relation to the enforcement proceedings can properly be characterised as so unreasonable or improper as to warrant a departure from costs being ordered on a party/party basis.
The issue then becomes whether in the exercise of my discretion it is just to make an order for costs on a party/party basis in a fixed amount or leave the parties to an assessment process. Rule 12.17 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”) and authorities make plain that I can fix an amount for costs. As McClelland DCJ observed in Pascoe & Larsen (No 2) [2022] FedCFamC1A 126:
Rule 12.17 of the Rules sets out the methods of calculating costs. These include the Court fixing a specific amount for costs (r 12.17(1)(a)) or an order for the costs to be assessed on a particular basis (r 12.17(1)(b)).
In Stoian v Fiening (Costs) [2014] FamCA 944 at [91], Kent J endorsed the principles for applying a rule equivalent to r 12.17 when referring to r 19.18 of the now repealed Family Law Rules 2004 (Cth) as adumbrated by Einstein J in Idoport Pty Limited v National Australia Bank Limited & Ors [2007] NSWSC 23 at [9]. Those principles include:
the purpose of the rule is to avoid the expense, delay and aggravation involved in protracted litigation arising out of taxation;...
the touchstone requires that the Court be confident that the approach taken to estimate costs is logical, fair and reasonable;...
the fairness parameter includes the Court having sufficient confidence in arriving at an appropriate sum on the materials available;...
a gross sum assessment, by its very nature, does not envisage that a process similar to that involved in a traditional taxation or assessment of costs should take place;...
the gross sum “can only be fixed broadly having regard to the information before the Court”;...
(Citations omitted)
Consistent with those principles, it has been determined that where a Court orders a party to pay costs, it may be appropriate for the Court to fix a lump sum. By doing so, the Court can avoid further delay and inconvenience being occasioned by the requirement to tax a bill: Byrnes v Brisconnections Management Co Ltd (No 2) [2009] FCA 1432 at [51].
In the present case the wife has not clearly identified the precise amount of costs said to have been incurred solely in relation to the Application – Enforcement. Whilst her costs notice provides some indication of the overall costs incurred in the proceedings, it does not isolate the costs attributable to the application the subject of this costs dispute.
Notwithstanding that deficiency, I am satisfied that it is appropriate to fix the wife’s costs in a lump sum. Doing so will avoid further delay, inconvenience and costs to the parties. I accept that it involved a broad assessment on the basis of the information before the Court. Doing the best I can, I am satisfied that an appropriate sum for the wife’s costs of and incidental to the Application – Enforcement is $5,000.
I am not persuaded that there is a sufficient basis to defer the enforceability of that order until the final determination of the proceedings. The Application – Enforcement arose from earlier orders of the court, and the wife should not be required to await the conclusion of already protracted proceedings in order to recover those costs.
Noting my reasons in Reynders & Travere (No 2) [2025] FedCFamC1F 797 and that the husband appeared represented by senior counsel, I am satisfied the husband has the capacity to meet such an order and will direct the husband to pay $5,000 to the wife within 28 days.

COSTS IN RELATION TO THE APPLICATION IN A PROCEEDING

The wife also seeks her costs in relation to her Application in a Proceeding filed on 28 January 2026, which was similarly listed for hearing on 9 February 2026. By that application, the wife sought orders in relation to the attempted registration of second mortgages over certain properties by the second respondent. The matter was resolved by consent on that day, and orders were made albeit not in the terms of the relief sought by the wife.
The wife seeks an order that the second respondent pay her costs on an indemnity basis fixed in the sum of $8,360. She contends that the application was necessitated by the second respondent’s conduct, including the attempted registration of the mortgages and failure to respond to correspondence, and that “the effect of the second respondent’s capitulation was that she was wholly unsuccessful” (wife’s written submissions filed 18 February 2026, paragraph 9).
The second respondent opposes any order for costs and submits that each party should bear their own costs. The second respondent contends that the matter was resolved by consent, that she acted reasonably throughout. She further submits that the issue of costs was not raised at the hearing when consent orders were made.
The Act provides that each party should pay their own costs unless there are justifying circumstances. It is not in issue that the application was resolved by consent. Both the Act and the Rules encourage parties to resolve issues by consent, with recourse to the Court intended as an avenue of last resort.
Whilst the wife contends that the application was necessitated by the conduct of the second respondent, that characterisation is disputed and there was no adjudication on the merits. It cannot therefore be assumed that the wife would have been successful had the matter proceeded. In Re Minister for Immigration and Ethnic Affairs; Ex parte Lai Qin (1997) 186 CLR 622 at 624, McHugh J observed that where litigation is determined without a hearing on the merits, courts should be reluctant to make costs orders which require consideration of a hypothetical hearing and outcome.
Courts should exercise caution in straying into an examining of the merits of applications that the parties have themselves chosen to compromise. In those circumstances, I am not satisfied that it is an appropriate case to depart from the usual position. The wife’s Application in a Proceeding filed 28 January 2026 will be dismissed.
I will make orders accordingly.
